examples: Each song rendition differs — improvisation over a structural template
Note selection from personal repertoire in real time
Parallels jazz solo performance: chord changes as framework, note choice as creative act
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Improvisation over form. The song template provides harmonic/structural constraints; the individual makes creative choices within them.

examples: Micro-pauses (10-50 ms) divide cascade into groups of 3-8 notes
Hierarchical nesting: notes within motifs, motifs within phrases, phrases within bout
Bar lines and phrase marks in notation serve identical grouping function
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Nested temporal organization — the same principle structuring bars, phrases, sections, and movements in composed music.

examples: Rapid pitch changes via syringeal muscle tension modulation at millisecond timescales
Chowning (1973) FM synthesis paper as technical parallel
Two oscillators (bilateral syrinx) each independently frequency-modulated
Moog voltage-controlled oscillators (1964) achieve electronically what the wren achieves biologically
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: FM synthesis — systematic variation of pitch over time to generate complex timbres from simple vibrating sources.

examples: Identifiable motifs recur with modifications (pitch transposition, rhythmic alteration, extension/truncation)
Bach Goldberg Variations, Beethoven Diabelli Variations — same formal structure
Balances repetition (recognition) with variation (novelty) to sustain interest over time
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Theme and variation — one of the oldest compositional structures, solving the universal problem of temporal coherence through patterned change.

examples: Rapid alternation between adjacent pitches — acoustically identical to keyboard trills
Grace notes (acciaccatura) and mordent-like turns visible in spectrograms
Maps directly to Baroque ornament tables (C.P.E. Bach, Quantz, Couperin)
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Ornamentation — decorative figures applied to structural notes. The wren's ornament vocabulary parallels Baroque performance practice.

examples: Left and right bronchial halves produce independent frequencies simultaneously
Non-harmonically-related dual traces visible in spectrograms (Suthers 1990)
Transition from monophony (degree 0 heron) to polyphony (degree 1 wren)
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Polyphony — multiple independent voices from a single source. The transition from Gregorian chant to organum, enacted by a 10-gram bird.

examples: 3-8 kHz frequency window optimized for understory propagation
High canopy blocks wind noise; dense understory buffers ambient sound
Forest IS the reverb chamber — degrade it and you degrade the acoustic channel
Lowest ambient noise levels of any terrestrial habitat
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Acoustic architecture — the performance space shapes the music. Concert hall acoustics, studio design, and forest structure all serve the same function.

examples: Song is learned from neighboring adults during a sensitive period (not innate)
Local dialects emerge from cultural transmission chains
Parallels human musical traditions: master-apprentice, oral tradition, regional style
theory_nodes.music_theory_parallel: Cultural transmission — music as learned tradition passed between generations. Local dialect = regional musical style.

cross_references.title: Wings of the Pacific Northwest
cross_references.connection: Passerine taxonomy and distribution; Pacific Wren species account

cross_references.title: Living Systems of the PNW
cross_references.connection: Old-growth forest ecology, understory structure, decay-stage wood habitat

cross_references.title: Bio-Physics Sensing
cross_references.connection: Bilateral syrinx mechanics, FM synthesis via muscle control, acoustic propagation in forests

cross_references.title: Columbia Valley
cross_references.connection: Old-growth remnants along Columbia tributaries supporting wren populations

cross_references.title: Seattle 360 Music
cross_references.connection: Part A parallel: Bill Frisell at degree 1 — chromatic elaboration, textural layering, complexity from simplicity

cross_references.title: Great Blue Heron (Degree 0)
cross_references.connection: Direct predecessor. Silence-to-complexity bridge. Monophony to polyphony, bass to soprano, geological tempo to millisecond precision.

part_a_parallel.shared_principle: Chromatic elaboration over foundational structure; textural complexity emerging from apparent simplicity; the cascade of notes over a bass pedal.
resonance_points: Textural layering: Frisell builds dense, shimmering textures from simple guitar lines — the wren builds dense cascades from a 10-gram body
Chromatic elaboration: unexpected intervals and pitch bends parallel the wren's frequency modulation and non-standard interval leaps
Complexity from apparent simplicity: both seem effortless until you try to transcribe what is actually happening
Foundational contrast: Frisell's music gains meaning from the silence and space around the notes — the wren's cascade gains meaning from the heron's silence at degree 0

silence_to_complexity_bridge.music_parallel: Whole note yields to sixty-fourth note cascade. Bass pedal gives way to melodic entrance. Cage yields to Coltrane. Jones yields to Frisell.

conservation.state_status: Monitored; old-growth dependency makes it a bioindicator of forest condition
primary_threats: Old-growth forest loss (80-90% reduction from pre-contact extent in PNW)
Timber harvest of mature second-growth before reaching old-growth characteristics
Forest fragmentation reducing contiguous understory habitat
Anthropogenic noise pollution degrading acoustic environment
conservation.habitat_note: Does not adapt to clearcuts or plantations. Requires structural complexity that only centuries of forest development produce.

key_sites.name: Discovery Park old-growth understory

key_sites.name: Seward Park old-growth
key_sites.description: One of the last old-growth fragments in urban Seattle

key_sites.name: Olympic National Park (Hoh Rain Forest)
key_sites.description: Pristine old-growth temperate rainforest; high Pacific Wren density
